items:
**Population:** The complete set of all individuals 
 or objects being studied.

**Sample:** A subset of the population that is actually 
 studied.

**Census:** Collecting data from every member of the 
 population.

**Random Sample:** Every member of the population has 
 an equal chance of being selected.

**Bias:** A systematic error in data collection that 
 favors certain outcomes.

**Mean:** The sum of all values divided by the number 
 of values.
 \[
 \text{Mean} = \frac{\text{Sum of all values}}{\text{Number of values}}
 \]

**Median:** The middle value when data is arranged in 
 order. For an even number of values, it is the average of the two 
 middle values.

**Mode:** The value that occurs most frequently. A data 
 set may have no mode, one mode, or multiple modes.

**Range:** The difference between the maximum and 
 minimum values.
 \[
 \text{Range} = \text{Maximum} - \text{Minimum}
 \]

**Bar Graph:** Uses bars to compare values across 
 categories.

**Line Graph:** Uses connected points to show change 
 over time.

**Circle Graph (Pie Chart):** Shows parts of a whole 
 using sectors of a circle. Each sector's central angle equals:
 \[
 \text{Central angle} = \frac{\text{Percentage}}{100} \times 360°
 \]

content.text: When to Use Each Measure of Center

rows: Data has no extreme outliers; you want to use all values
Data has outliers or is skewed; you want a "typical" value
Data is categorical; you want the most common value

content.text: When to Use Each Type of Graph

rows: Comparing two sets of data across the same categories
Showing change over time or continuous trends
Showing parts of a whole (percentages that add to 100\%)

content.text: *Sections 10.1–10.2: Data Collection and Measures of Center*
